paragraphs: Sandpaper Fig is Ficus coronata, a small cauliflorous fig native to eastern Australia, found from central Queensland through New South Wales and just into Victoria. It is a small tree, often 6 to 12m, growing along river banks and gullies in rainforest and open forest. Its common name comes from its scabrous, sandpapery upper leaf surface; the ovate leaves are about 5 to 15cm long.

The small oval fruit, around 1.5cm long and covered in fine hairs, is edible and sweet, often rated among the best-tasting native figs. The figs are eaten fresh once the furry skin is removed, or dried and cooked into pies, jams, and similar. A well-known traditional use of the rough leaves was as a natural abrasive for smoothing and polishing wooden tools, spears, and boomerangs.

claims.text: Sandpaper Fig is Ficus coronata, a small cauliflorous fig native to eastern Australia from central Queensland through NSW into Victoria
It reaches about 6 to 12m along riverbanks and gullies, with scabrous sandpapery leaves about 5 to 15cm long
The hairy oval fruit about 1.5cm long is edible and sweet, eaten fresh once the furry skin is removed or cooked into preserves
The rough leaves were traditionally used as an abrasive to smooth and polish wooden tools, spears and boomerangs

sources.name: The Conversation: Sandpaper figs make food, fire, medicine and a cosy home for wasps
